173.Option: The study provides definitive evidence that dengue fever increases the risk of severe pregnancy outcomes in all pregnant women, regardless of gestational age or prior immunity.
Evidence: Among 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission
Of 76 patients with dengue, 71/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion; 16/57 (28%) had primary dengue and 41/57 (72%) had secondary dengue.
173.Rationale: The study shows an association between dengue and severe outcomes in the recruited hospitalized women, but it does not provide 'definitive evidence' for all pregnant women regardless of gestational age or prior immunity. The data includes mixed infections and no adjustment for confounders; moreover, the study design does not allow for causal inferences or generalization to all pregnant women without limitations. The Passage Body does not analyze outcomes by gestational age or prior immunity in a way that supports this broad claim.

173.Option: The high frequency of dengue in pregnant women admitted with fever suggests that dengue is the most common cause of fever in the general population of Vientiane, Laos.
Evidence: Among those (132, 53%) who had a single disease, dengue was the most commonly found (76 (30%))

The hospital-based prospective study was conducted in Mahosot Hospital and the Mother and Child Hospital, Vientiane, Laos between February 2006 and November 2010. We recruited all pregnant women admitted with tympanic temperature ≥37.5°C who provided written informed consent.
173.Rationale: The study only includes pregnant women admitted to hospital with fever, which is a specific subgroup. Inferring that dengue is the most common cause of fever in the general population—which includes non-pregnant individuals, children, and those with mild or asymptomatic infections not requiring hospitalization—is an ecological fallacy and not supported by the Passage Body. The data does not extend to the general population.

174.Option: The 2011–2012 cluster of closely related genotype 1.1 isolates likely resulted from a single introduction of a new S. Typhi strain from a neighboring country during the period of hyperendemic transmission.
Evidence: The S. Typhi isolates from Santiago of genotypes 3.5, 2, 1.1 and 1.2.1 clustered together and were genetically distinct from those sequenced previously from other countries.
the twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases
174.Rationale: The passage indicates that the genotype 1.1 isolates from Santiago are phylogenetically distinct from those found in other countries, arguing against a recent introduction from a neighboring country. The low SNP distance within the 2011-2012 cluster suggests a recent common ancestor, but the evidence points to local persistence (possibly from a chronic carrier) rather than an introduction during the hyperendemic period.

174.Option: The persistence of ancient S. Typhi clades in Santiago, Chile, despite the interruption of hyperendemic transmission, suggests that asymptomatic typhoid carriers are likely responsible for ongoing sporadic cases.
Evidence: The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.
The genomic population structure of S. Typhi in Santiago appeared highly consistent between the hyperendemic and post-hyperendemic eras.

the twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.
The intermingling of historical and recent sequences suggests that the 2010s isolates originated from progenitors within the same pool of S. Typhi circulating in the 1980s.
174.Rationale: The passage explicitly lists chronic carriers as a source of sporadic cases. Genomic data show that modern isolates are phylogenetically intermingled with historical isolates from the hyperendemic era, indicating persistence of ancient clades. The low genetic variation in the 2011-2012 cluster is inconsistent with acute transmission and suggests a chronic carrier as the source, supporting the inference that asymptomatic carriers are responsible for ongoing sporadic cases.

174.Option: The detection of a novel variant of plasmid pHCM2 in two genotype 1.2.1 isolates from Santiago suggests that this plasmid may play a role in the persistence of S. Typhi in the human host.
Evidence: Two pHCM2 plasmids were found among our isolates from genotype 1.2.1; one in a 1984 isolate (ERR2710544) and the other from 2013 (ERR4289185). These plasmids were highly similar to each other, but significantly different from the pHCM2 found on S. Typhi reference CT18
174.Rationale: While the passage describes a novel variant of plasmid pHCM2 in two isolates, it does not provide any evidence linking this plasmid to persistence in the human host. No epidemiological or functional data are presented to support a causal role; the observation is purely descriptive, and thus the inference cannot be derived from the passage.

174.Option: The global dominance of the H58 S. Typhi clade in other industrialized nations suggests that imported cases are the primary source of sporadic typhoid in Santiago, Chile.
Evidence: The majority of publicly available sequenced isolates come from hyperendemic regions in South Asia and East Africa where H58 (4.3.1) genotypes dominate.
These include three previously described importations of isolates belonging to H58 genotypes 4.3.1.1 and 4.3.1.2.

The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.
174.Rationale: The passage notes that H58 genotypes dominate in other regions but are only rarely imported into Santiago (three importations). The listing of sources for sporadic cases indicates that imported cases are one of several sources, not the primary one. Genomic data show that most modern isolates are from local, non-H58 genotypes that persist from the hyperendemic era, undermining the claim that imports are the primary source.

174.Option: The low genetic diversity observed in the 2011–2012 genotype 1.1 cluster indicates that the S. Typhi population in Santiago has undergone a recent bottleneck event, leading to the loss of most ancestral genotypes.
Evidence: The genomic population structure of S. Typhi in Santiago appeared highly consistent between the hyperendemic and post-hyperendemic eras.
most of the post-2010 sporadic S. Typhi isolates shared bootstrap-supported phylogenetic clusters with the 1980 hyperendemic isolates.
the twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs
174.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that the overall genotypic composition has remained consistent over time, with multiple ancestral genotypes persisting into the modern era. The low diversity in the specific 2011-2012 cluster is attributed to a recent common ancestor (possibly a chronic carrier), not a population-wide bottleneck. There is no evidence of loss of ancestral genotypes; in fact, the population structure is highly consistent.

175.Option: Ergot alkaloids are efficacious modulators of planarian regeneration and motility, and their structural-activity relationships suggest potential for developing novel chemotherapeutics targeting parasite muscle function.
Evidence: Ergot alkaloids are a historically important class of compounds that realize their effects because of the close structural similarity of the ergoline scaffold to bioaminergic transmitters. Numerous ergot compounds yielded regenerative phenotypes, either phenocopying PZQ to promote bipolar (‘two-head’) regeneration or inhibiting head regeneration (‘no-head’, Fig 3A), all at doses lower than PZQ.

This broad efficacy of ergot alkaloids as a chemical class permitted structural-activity insight into features associated with specific polarity effects: for example, all ergots that caused bipolarity were either alkylated on the indole nitrogen or halogenated at the adjacent 2-position (Fig 3A).
Therefore, ergot alkaloids possess efficacy against schistosomules, with an action predictable by planarian polarity outcomes.
Based on these data, we contend that the ergot alkaloid scaffold merits further exploration to yield novel chemotherapeutics with selective efficacy against parasite musculature.
175.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that ergot alkaloids modulate planarian regeneration and motility, showing specific structural-activity relationships. It also demonstrates efficacy against schistosomules and explicitly states that the ergot scaffold merits further exploration for developing new drugs targeting parasite muscle function. This inference is fully derivable from the experimental findings described.

175.Option: The structural-activity relationships identified in planarian regenerative screens are sufficient to guarantee the development of parasite-specific drugs with minimal off-target effects in humans.
175.Rationale: The passage discusses structural-activity relationships for ergot alkaloids in planarian screens but does not claim that these relationships are sufficient to guarantee successful drug development or minimal off-target effects in humans. It only suggests potential for further exploration, and no evidence is provided regarding guarantees, specificity, or human safety. This option overstates the findings and requires assumptions not supported by the passage.

175.Option: Since PZQ’s mechanism of action is unresolved, the discovery that ergot alkaloids mimic its effects in planarians suggests that PZQ’s primary target is the serotonergic system in flatworms.
Evidence: the mechanism of action of PZQ remains unresolved
PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.
175.Rationale: While the passage confirms that PZQ's mechanism is unresolved and shows functional interactions with serotonergic signaling (e.g., ergot alkaloids mimic some effects, and 5-HT antagonizes PZQ), it does not infer that PZQ's primary target is the serotonergic system. The evidence suggests a link but does not establish a direct target; the mechanism remains unresolved, and the passage does not draw this conclusion.

175.Option: Because ergot alkaloids are already used clinically for migraine, obstetrics, and Parkinson’s disease, they are likely to be safe and effective for treating schistosomiasis without requiring further development.
175.Rationale: The passage does not mention any clinical uses of ergot alkaloids for migraine, obstetrics, or Parkinson’s disease. It only describes their effects in planarian and schistosomule assays. There is no information provided about their safety, efficacy, or development status for schistosomiasis. This inference relies entirely on external knowledge and cannot be derived from the passage.

175.Option: The functional antagonism between serotonergic signaling and PZQ action suggests that PZQ may directly target flatworm bioaminergic receptors as an ergomimetic, which could be confirmed through heterologous expression of platyhelminth GpCRs.
Evidence: the bipolarizing action of PZQ was blocked by co-incubation with 5-HT
PZQ action is functionally antagonized by serotonergic activation in schistosomule motility experiments
175.Rationale: The passage demonstrates functional antagonism between serotonergic signaling and PZQ action, but it does not suggest that PZQ directly targets bioaminergic receptors as an ergomimetic. While ergot alkaloids are described as ergomimetics, PZQ is not equated to them in this way. The mention of heterologous expression is not proposed in the passage as a method to confirm PZQ's target; it is only used for deorphanizing receptors. This option makes a speculative leap not supported by the evidence.

176.Option: The decision tree algorithm for prognostication achieved high positive predictive value in low-prevalence settings, making it ideal for identifying severe dengue cases in non-outbreak periods.
176.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about positive predictive value, prevalence settings, or non-outbreak periods. The study reports sensitivity and specificity but not PPV. The prevalence in the study cohort is approximately 30% (364 dengue out of 1200), and no analysis of different prevalence settings is presented. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

176.Option: The decision tree model for dengue diagnosis is more effective than traditional laboratory tests such as RT-PCR and ELISA in early detection of dengue infection.
176.Rationale: The passage mentions that traditional laboratory tests like RT-PCR and ELISA have limitations (cost, speed, availability), but it does not compare the effectiveness of the decision tree model to these tests. The decision tree's sensitivity (71.2%) and specificity (90.1%) are reported, but no direct comparison is made to establish superiority over RT-PCR or ELISA, which are considered definitive diagnostic methods. Therefore, this inference is not supported by the passage.

176.Option: The use of a decision tree approach can differentiate dengue from non-dengue febrile illness and predict disease outcome using simple haematological and virological parameters obtained within the first 72 hours of illness.
Evidence: we show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.
We also demonstrate a proof-of-concept that such an approach can be useful for early dengue disease prognostication.
The resulting diagnostic algorithm is shown in Figure 1.

The best performing decision algorithm made used of platelet count, the crossover value (Ct) of the real-time RT-PCR for dengue viral RNA (a marker for viremia levels) and the presence of anti-dengue IgG antibodies, as the first, second and third splitting parameters, respectively (Figure 3).
176.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the decision tree approach can distinguish dengue from non-dengue using clinical and haematological parameters within the first 72 hours. It also demonstrates that the same approach can predict disease severity (prognostication) using parameters like platelet count, viral load (Ct), and IgG status obtained at the first visit. These statements and the results presented directly support the inference.

176.Option: The use of a decision tree approach allows for the integration of dynamic laboratory parameters into a single predictive model, enabling real-time clinical decision-making during dengue outbreaks.
176.Rationale: The passage does not mention 'dynamic' laboratory parameters; the decision tree uses parameters from a single time point (first visit). While it integrates multiple parameters into a model, the term 'dynamic' implies serial measurements or changes over time, which are not described. Additionally, 'real-time clinical decision-making' and 'during dengue outbreaks' are not explicitly discussed. The passage focuses on early diagnosis and prognostication in general, not specifically during outbreaks. Therefore, this inference cannot be directly derived from the passage.

176.Option: The lack of evidence-based diagnostic algorithm for early dengue diagnosis as well as prognostic triage strategies limits effective patient management, use of healthcare resources and disease surveillance efforts.
Evidence: Dengue illness is often confused with other viral febrile states, confounding both clinical management [4]–[6] and disease surveillance for viral transmission prevention [7].

Evidence-based triage strategies that identify individuals likely to have dengue infection in the early stages of illness are needed to direct patient stratification in clinical investigations, management and healthcare resource planning.

The current World Health Organization (WHO) scheme for classifying dengue infection (Table S1) makes use of symptoms and signs that are often not present in the first few days of illness, and thus not a guide for early diagnosis, but are instead designed for monitoring disease progression for clinical management of the severe DHF/DSS.
176.Rationale: The passage clearly describes the limitations of current diagnostic and triage strategies, stating that the lack of evidence-based algorithms for early diagnosis and prognostication hampers clinical management, healthcare resource planning, and disease surveillance. These statements directly support the inference that such a lack limits effectiveness.

177.Option: Surveillance methods with greater sensitivity and more targeted sampling strategies to better detect residual hotspots than the current TAS methodology will be required.
Evidence: It is acknowledged that the TAS methodology is resource-intensive and may also lack sensitivity in low-prevalence settings.
Table 6 which shows that alternative surveillance methods can identify evidence to support ongoing transmission in areas which passed TAS.
For example, Sheel et al. report LF prevalence (using Filarial Test Strips) of 6.2% in a community survey in an area, which had recently passed TAS[14].
177.Rationale: The passage directly states that the TAS methodology lacks sensitivity in low-prevalence settings and provides evidence from studies where alternative surveillance methods detected ongoing transmission in areas that had passed TAS. This implies that future monitoring systems need more sensitive and targeted approaches to detect residual hotspots, which is a logical epidemiological inference from the evidence presented.

177.Option: Because mosquito surveillance provides a snapshot of current infection and is less affected by the persistence of markers in the host, it should replace human-based surveillance in all post-validation settings.
Evidence: Overall, there was great variability in survey methods and results which limited comparisons.
There were also examples where human testing did not detect significant transmission but xenomonitoring did.
177.Rationale: While the passage discusses mosquito surveillance (xenomonitoring) and notes instances where it detected transmission when human testing did not, it does not claim that mosquito surveillance is less affected by marker persistence or that it should replace human surveillance entirely. The passage presents both methods as complementary and highlights variability without making definitive recommendations for replacement. This inference cannot be derived solely from the passage body.

177.Option: The use of antibody tests such as Wb123 Ab may be particularly useful in post-validation settings due to their early positivity and faster decay after infection clearance, making them more suitable for detecting current transmission than antigen tests.
Evidence: Bm14Ab and Wb123Ab values are, on average, 5.1 and 6.7 times higher respectively than the corresponding BinaxNOW values, based on the median value of this ratio across the selected studies.
177.Rationale: The passage compares prevalence values of different diagnostic tests, showing that antibody tests yield higher positivity rates. However, it does not mention or imply anything about early positivity, faster decay after infection clearance, or suitability for detecting current transmission. These concepts are not addressed in the passage, so the inference requires external knowledge and cannot be derived from the evidence provided.

177.Option: The increased risk of disease recrudescence in post-validation settings means that countries should immediately resume mass drug administration (MDA) upon detection of any positive case in surveillance.
Evidence: Following validation of elimination of LF as a public health problem, the WHO recommend that countries continue surveillance for LF to detect any possible recrudescence of infection but there are no clear recommendations on specific surveillance methods and thresholds to be used.
177.Rationale: The passage mentions the need for surveillance to detect recrudescence but does not discuss or imply any action regarding resuming MDA upon detection of a positive case. There is no evidence provided about response protocols or the immediate resumption of MDA. This inference is beyond the scope of the passage and cannot be derived from epidemiological reasoning based on the text.

178.Option: The current WHO monitoring protocol for LF elimination is sufficient to support the detection of system-level changes in parasite dynamics, as it aligns with the time points at which significant reductions in model uncertainty occur.
Evidence: The available mf prevalence data from each site were arranged into four different temporal data streams to imitate the current WHO guidelines regarding the time points for conducting monitoring surveys during an MDA programme.
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.
The inclusion of more data than are considered in scenario 3 therefore did not result in any significant additional reduction in model uncertainty.
178.Rationale: The WHO monitoring protocol specifies data collection at baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5, which corresponds to scenario 3. The passage shows that scenario 3 most often reduces model uncertainty the greatest, and adding more data beyond these time points does not significantly improve predictions. This implies that the protocol provides sufficient data for constraining models to detect changes in parasite dynamics through improved predictive accuracy.

178.Option: The most accurate predictions of LF elimination timelines are achieved when models are calibrated using data collected at the time of the first post-MDA survey, as this early data point provides the strongest constraint on model parameters.
Evidence: data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2).
178.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that data from later time points, specifically post-MDA 5, provides greater reduction in model uncertainty compared to earlier data like the first post-MDA survey (post-MDA 3). Therefore, the first post-MDA survey does not offer the strongest constraint, and models calibrated with later data yield more accurate predictions.

178.Option: Model predictions of LF elimination timelines are most sensitive to the initial baseline infection data, and additional longitudinal data have minimal impact on reducing prediction uncertainty.
Evidence: models constrained by data provided significantly more precise intervention predictions compared to model-only predictions
model-only predictions made in the absence of data (scenario 0) showed the highest prediction uncertainty

data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2)
178.Rationale: The passage clearly indicates that adding longitudinal data beyond baseline significantly reduces prediction uncertainty, with post-MDA 5 data having the greatest impact. Baseline data alone is insufficient, and additional data substantially improves model precision, contradicting the claim that additional data has minimal impact.

178.Option: The use of longitudinal data from at least five years post-MDA is sufficient to constrain model predictions of the impact of LF interventions and detect qualitative changes in system behavior, with additional data beyond this point not leading to significant further improvements in predictive performance.
Evidence: scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.
The inclusion of more data than are considered in scenario 3 therefore did not result in any significant additional reduction in model uncertainty.
178.Rationale: Scenario 3 includes data up to post-MDA 5, which corresponds to at least five years of annual MDA. The passage shows that this data stream is sufficient for significantly reducing model uncertainty, and that adding more data points beyond this (e.g., post-MDA 7 or 9) does not lead to statistically significant further improvements. This implies that data from five years post-MDA is adequate for constraining models and informing predictions.

178.Option: The optimal duration of longitudinal surveillance for parasite elimination models is determined by the length of the MDA program itself, with data collection required for the full duration of interventions to ensure accurate forecasts.
Evidence: MDA simulations were extended for a total of 50 annual rounds in each site at an assumed coverage of 65%.
scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.
178.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that accurate forecasts can be made using data from specific time points (e.g., baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5) without requiring data collection for the entire duration of the MDA program. Models are calibrated with limited data and used to predict long-term outcomes, indicating that full-duration surveillance is not necessary for reliable predictions.

179.Option: The under-diagnosis of zoonoses is mainly due to a lack of integration between animal and human healthcare sectors, which prevents the sharing of case information and limits cross-sectoral awareness.
179.Rationale: The passage does not identify lack of integration between animal and human healthcare sectors as a main factor for under-diagnosis. While it compares awareness between sectors, it does not provide evidence that sharing case information or cross-sectoral integration would address the issue. The factors highlighted are lack of knowledge, nonspecific presentations, and limited diagnostic capacity.

179.Option: The high visibility and dramatic clinical presentations of diseases like anthrax, rabies, and Ebola virus disease lead healthcare providers to prioritize these conditions over less conspicuous zoonoses such as brucellosis, leptospirosis, and Q fever.
179.Rationale: The passage mentions that rabies is widely known (all respondents named it) and anthrax is occasionally mentioned, but it does not discuss prioritization based on clinical presentation. No evidence is provided that dramatic presentations lead healthcare providers to overlook other zoonoses. The under-diagnosis is attributed to lack of awareness, nonspecific symptoms, and diagnostic limitations, not prioritization of certain diseases.

179.Option: Persistent over-diagnosis of malaria is primarily driven by a lack of confidence in the accuracy of malaria rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs), which leads healthcare providers to default to clinical diagnosis and overlook other causes of fever.
Evidence: Twelve (20%) respondents indicated a lack of confidence in malaria rapid diagnostic tests (mRDTs), and 11 (19%) insisted that sometimes mRDTs produced false negative malaria results when other tests had shown ‘true’ positives.

I have been working here for a long time and never got malaria positive using the mRDT test, though another hospital says they get positive malaria using the mRDT test. But I remember in the past while working with the other hospital, when mRDT showed negative for malaria, I advised them to use the microscope to test and the results would be positive for malaria, so I think people should not only rely on the mRDT test.
179.Rationale: While lack of confidence in mRDTs is mentioned as a factor contributing to malaria over-diagnosis, the passage does not establish it as the primary driver. Other factors such as healthcare providers' lack of knowledge about zoonoses, habitual assumption that fever indicates malaria, and patient pressure are also highlighted. The evidence does not support that this factor is the main explanation for persistent under-diagnosis of zoonoses.

179.Option: The non-specific clinical presentation of many zoonoses, combined with limited diagnostic testing capacity, contributes to a failure to recognize and diagnose these diseases in clinical practice.
Evidence: Clinical recognition of many zoonoses is made difficult by their nonspecific clinical presentations that overlap with other febrile illnesses.

It is widely recognized that limited testing capacity (considering both test availability and performance) in sub-Saharan Africa severely limits the options for accurate laboratory diagnostic testing for zoonoses.
A lack of testing capacity was also identified as playing a crucial role in the apparent under-diagnosis of zoonoses.

People come to the hospital with fever, but when you test they don’t have malaria, then what do they have? You start to guess, but if we had facilities to test diseases then you could test and confirm.
179.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that nonspecific clinical presentations make zoonoses difficult to recognize and that limited diagnostic testing capacity severely restricts accurate diagnosis. These factors are repeatedly emphasized as key reasons for under-diagnosis, supported by both the background and interview data. This option directly summarizes evidence from the passage.

179.Option: The low frequency of reported zoonoses cases in the past year is due to the fact that most patients with zoonotic infections do not seek healthcare, resulting in under-ascertainment of disease burden.
179.Rationale: The passage does not discuss healthcare-seeking behavior as a factor for low reported cases. The study focuses on inpatients who did seek healthcare, and under-diagnosis is documented among those presenting. The low frequency of reported cases is attributed to lack of awareness, diagnostic limitations, and misattribution to other diseases, not to patients not seeking care.

180.Option: Cx43 plays a key role in cell proliferation, potentially leading to cholangiocarcinoma development upon stimulation by excretory-secretory products of Clonorchis sinensis and the carcinogen NDMA.
Evidence: Proliferation was higher in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP of C. sinensis than in control cells.
The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells.
Downregulation of Cx43 by Cx43-specific small interfering RNA resulted in a reduction in Cx43 expression.
Real-time PCR showed that Cx43 silencing resulted in the downregulation of Cx26 and Cox-2.
180.Rationale: The experimental findings show that Cx43 is upregulated in cells exposed to NDMA and ESP, and its silencing reduces cell proliferation and downregulates cancer-related markers like Cox-2. This indicates Cx43 is involved in promoting cell proliferation, a key step in carcinogenesis, supporting the inference that it plays a role in leading to cholangiocarcinoma development.

180.Option: The upregulation of Cx43 expression in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP of C. sinensis is likely an adaptive protective response of the liver to enable better cell-to-cell communication.
Evidence: The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells.
180.Rationale: While Cx43 is upregulated, the passage associates this with increased cell proliferation and cancer-related changes, not with a protective response. Silencing Cx43 reduces proliferation, suggesting its upregulation contributes to harmful effects rather than serving as adaptive protection.

180.Option: The inhibition of Cox-2 expression following Cx43 knockdown suggests that Cx43 functions as a direct suppressor of inflammatory signaling in cholangiocytes exposed to NDMA and ESP.
Evidence: Real-time PCR showed that Cx43 silencing resulted in the downregulation of Cx26 and Cox-2.
180.Rationale: Cox-2 is a pro-inflammatory molecule. Since Cx43 knockdown reduces Cox-2 expression, it indicates that Cx43 promotes Cox-2 expression and inflammatory signaling. If Cx43 were a suppressor, its knockdown would be expected to increase inflammatory markers, not decrease them.

180.Option: The downregulation of Cx32 expression in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP indicates that Cx32 acts as a tumor suppressor in the context of Clonorchis sinensis-induced carcinogenesis.
Evidence: The expression of Cx32 (blue) was markedly lower in cells treated with NDMA + ESP than in other cells.
In hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), for instance, reduced Cx32 expression is accompanied by increased expression of Cx43, which promotes HCC via cell-to-cell communication.
Cx32 has a suppressive effect in metastatic renal cell carcinoma.
180.Rationale: The experimental finding shows Cx32 is downregulated in cells exposed to carcinogenic stimuli. Combined with background information indicating Cx32 has tumor-suppressive effects in other cancers, it can be inferred that Cx32 likely acts as a tumor suppressor in this context, and its downregulation may contribute to carcinogenesis.

180.Option: The synergistic increase in cell proliferation and G2/M phase progression observed in H69 cells treated with NDMA and ESP is primarily driven by the upregulation of Cx26 rather than Cx43.
Evidence: The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells.
Cx43 silencing resulted in the downregulation of Cx26 and Cox-2.
180.Rationale: Both Cx26 and Cx43 are upregulated, but Cx43 silencing reduces proliferation and affects Cx26 expression, suggesting that Cx43 plays a central role. The passage does not provide evidence that Cx26 is the primary driver; in fact, Cx43 seems to influence Cx26, indicating Cx43 might be more critical in the observed cellular changes.

181.Option: The high compliance with repeated PZQ treatments and the low frequency of adverse events suggest that multiple doses of praziquantel are both safe and highly effective in eliminating Schistosoma mansoni infections in school-aged children.
Evidence: compliance to each following treatment was unexpectedly high, from 100% in week 2 (second treatment) to 98% in week 6 (fourth and final treatment).
Adverse events decreased with subsequent treatments in the intense treatment group.
Most of the adverse events were mild and all of them resolved 24 hours after treatment.
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group.
181.Rationale: While compliance and safety data are supported, the inference about high effectiveness in eliminating infections is not solely derivable from the comparison of diagnostic methods, which is the focus of the question. Moreover, the POC-CCA test shows significantly lower cure rates than KK, indicating persistent active infections and challenging the claim of 'highly effective' elimination. Thus, this option does not logically follow from the diagnostic comparison alone.

181.Option: Since the POC-CCA test detects active infections and the KK technique detects eggs, the higher cure rate observed with KK must be due to the test's greater specificity, indicating that PZQ is highly effective in killing adult worms.
Evidence: reported CRs based on these parasitological methods are likely an overestimation
the POC-CCA test, which identifies active worm infections by detection of schistosome CCA in urine, has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique.
POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique
181.Rationale: The passage indicates that KK lacks sensitivity, especially for low-intensity infections, leading to overestimation of cure rates. POC-CCA is more sensitive, so the higher cure rate with KK is likely due to its lower sensitivity missing persistent infections, not greater specificity. The passage does not support that KK has greater specificity or that PZQ is 'highly effective' based on this comparison; instead, it suggests efficacy may be overestimated by KK.

181.Option: The use of the POC-CCA test revealed a significantly lower cure rate compared to the KK technique, indicating that PZQ may be less efficacious than previously reported due to persistent active infections despite treatment.
Evidence: POC-CCA-based CRs were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group.
reported CRs based on these parasitological methods are likely an overestimation
the POC-CCA test... has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique.
181.Rationale: The direct comparison of diagnostic methods shows that POC-CCA, which is more sensitive for active infections, yields lower cure rates than KK. This discrepancy suggests that KK-based reports may overestimate PZQ efficacy, and persistent infections detected by POC-CCA indicate that PZQ might be less efficacious than previously thought. This inference is logically derived from the evidence presented in the passage.

181.Option: The discrepancy between KK and POC-CCA results suggests that the POC-CCA test is unreliable for detecting Schistosoma mansoni infections in post-treatment settings, particularly in children with low-intensity infections.
Evidence: It is now being recommended for surveillance and mapping of prevalence of intestinal schistosomiasis.
the POC-CCA test... has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique.
the proportion of POC-CCA negatives (including traces) increased over time, particularly in the intense treatment group.
181.Rationale: The passage recommends POC-CCA for surveillance due to its higher sensitivity, and the discrepancy in results is attributed to KK's lower sensitivity, not POC-CCA's unreliability. There is no evidence suggesting POC-CCA is unreliable; instead, it is presented as a more accurate tool for detecting active infections, especially in low-intensity settings. the rMFI ratio of ZIKV NS1 to DENV2 NS1; interestingly a cutoff of the rMFI ratio at 0.62 was able to distinguish these two panels with a sensitivity of 94.4% and specificity of 90.9%

229.Rationale: While the passage states that leptospirosis outbreaks can occur after hurricanes and floods, it does not provide evidence that urban residents in USVI have greater exposure to floodwater or standing water compared to other areas. The association with urban residence was found in bivariate analysis, but no causal link to floodwater exposure is established from the passage. This interpretation requires external assumptions not supported by the data presented.

229.Option: The association between urban residence and leptospirosis seropositivity suggests that urban environments in USVI may have higher levels of environmental contamination with Leptospira spp. due to increased rodent populations and poor infrastructure.
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03)
seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01)
229.Rationale: Although rodent exposure is associated with seropositivity, the passage does not provide evidence that urban areas have higher rodent populations or poor infrastructure. The interpretation that urban environments may have higher contamination due to these factors is speculative and not directly derived from the passage. The association with urban residence is noted, but no causal mechanisms are explained in the text.

229.Option: Urban residents in USVI are more likely to have been exposed to Leptospira spp. through contact with domestic animals such as cows and rodents, which are more prevalent in urban settings.
Evidence: contact with cows or their body fluids (pRR: 14.0; 95% CI: 5.6–35.2; p<0.001)
seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01)
229.Rationale: The passage shows that contact with cows and rodents is associated with seropositivity, but it does not state that these animals are more prevalent in urban settings. In fact, cows are typically associated with rural areas. This interpretation assumes a distribution of animals not supported by the passage, making it not derivable from the evidence provided.

229.Option: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
Evidence: Neighborhood type was subjectively assigned by interviewers as rural, urban, or suburban at the time of survey–using the following guidelines: urban if within a major city, suburban if somewhat populated and primarily residential, and rural if sparsely populated with unpaved roads.
Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03)

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001); seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids (OR = 2.6, 95% CI = 1.1–5.9, p = 0.02); and increasing age (OR = 1.02, 95% CI = 1.002–1.04, p = 0.03).
229.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that neighborhood classification was subjective, which can introduce measurement bias. Moreover, urban residence was significant in bivariate analysis but not included in the multivariate logistic regression results, indicating that it did not remain significant when adjusted for other factors like animal contact and age. This suggests that the association may be confounded, and cautious interpretation is warranted based on epidemiological principles directly supported by the evidence.

229.Option: The higher seropositivity among urban residents reflects a true geographic disparity in leptospirosis risk, indicating that urban areas in USVI should be prioritized for targeted public health interventions.
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03)

Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids; seeing rodents/evidence of rodents or contact with rodents or their body fluids; and increasing age.
229.Rationale: While urban residence was associated with seropositivity in bivariate analysis, it was not an independent risk factor in multivariate analysis, suggesting that the association may be due to confounding variables like animal contact. The passage does not conclude that urban areas have a true geographic disparity or should be prioritized for interventions. This interpretation overstates the evidence and ignores the lack of significance in multivariate analysis, making it not derivable from the passage.

230.Option: The real-time surveillance system demonstrated in this study relies on a centralized, paper-based reporting framework that is highly efficient and error-prone, as evidenced by the high completion rates and remote monitoring of data.
Evidence: Paper forms were distributed as a backup alternative to mHealth data collection (e.g. in case of cell phone loss or failure).

The survey, which was applied once weekly, included information on coded household location, number of NS children/household, seizure frequency, availability of anti-seizure medication, and injuries and deaths.
Data collection via the Magpi’s mobile platform took place for 12 consecutive weeks
The mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week
230.Rationale: The system is primarily digital, using smartphones and Magpi software for data collection and transmission. Paper forms are only a backup, not the main framework. The high completion rates and remote monitoring are achieved through the digital system, not a paper-based one. The term 'error-prone' is not supported; while errors occurred, they were detected and corrected through remote monitoring.

230.Option: The use of GPS stamps and timestamped data in the Magpi system ensures that all health events are accurately geolocated and temporally precise, enabling perfect spatial and temporal mapping of disease outbreaks in real time.
Evidence: it became readily apparent that many geotracers malfunctioned or, most likely, many remote areas had weak or no coverage of cellular communication, causing limitations in acquiring GPS stamps and compromising the ability to produce maps using GPS coordinates.

Family absences during the harvest season forced mHealth reporters to follow the subjects to their gardens. This not only slowed the process of data collection but also changed some GPS coordinates, introducing undesired variability and therefore hindering the ability to produce maps using GPS stamps.
230.Rationale: The passage explicitly states limitations in acquiring accurate GPS stamps due to technical and logistical issues, and that this compromised mapping. The claim of 'perfect' spatial and temporal mapping is contradicted by these limitations.

230.Option: The use of minimally trained lay mHealth reporters with basic smartphones enabled real-time monitoring of neurological health data in remote rural communities, allowing for near-real-time identification of disease hotspots, medication shortages, injuries, and deaths with a delay of no more than 1–7 days.
Evidence: Eight male and female Ugandan lay mHealth reporters, age 20–30 years, were recruited and trained.
Inexpensive, locally purchased airtime-loaded smartphones (MTN Smart Mega; approx. $30/phone) supported by the local telecommunication company MTN, were individually programmed with Magpi software
Their weekly reports, transmitted by simple smartphones, were instantaneously aggregated, analyzed and mapped by data collection centers locally (Gulu, Uganda) and remotely (Oregon, USA).
The survey, which was applied once weekly
With the exception of one reporter ... the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week

All data acquired and integrated into the Magpi database could be exported, with no particular conversion problems, from the platform into statistical (Excel) or spatial (Magpi maps) analysis software.

Fig 2 displays ... real-time electronic map of child health, injury and illness relating to NS ... the close-up in Fig 2 highlights those households with at least one child with NS that did not have anti-seizure medication ... was injured ... or died that week.

During the 12-week data collection period, any mistyping, duplicated reports, data inconsistencies or doubtful information were reported by phone or email to the field coordinator ... for double-checking, fixing, and resending if necessary.
230.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that lay reporters with minimal training used basic smartphones to collect weekly health data on Nodding Syndrome. The data were transmitted and aggregated almost instantly, allowing for near-real-time mapping and identification of issues like medication shortages, injuries, and deaths. Since surveys were weekly, the delay between event occurrence and detection is at most 7 days, and the system enabled timely monitoring and response.

230.Option: The high survey completion rate of 97.3% indicates that the mHealth system is fully independent of network connectivity and can operate without any delays in data transmission, even in areas with poor infrastructure.
Evidence: The most common technical issues were frequent power cuts, poor network coverage, slow upload speeds and unreliable GPS satellites.

Inconsistent connectivity led to difficulties in survey uploading (e.g., some mHealth workers reported traveling to a specific hotspot or even climbing a particular tree to access satisfactory signal strength and network coverage).

Although the surveys collected during this period were stored on the memory of the cell phone and could have been uploaded later, some reporters stopped working when they realized their data bundles were exhausted.
Magpi supports ... off-line data collection (data can be collected off-line and submitted to a central server whenever an active connection becomes available)
230.Rationale: The high completion rate was achieved despite network connectivity issues, not because the system is independent of it. The passage clearly describes challenges with network coverage and the need for reporters to seek connectivity. The system allows offline data collection, but transmission still requires connectivity, and delays occurred due to these issues.

230.Option: The mHealth system's success in data collection and monitoring is primarily due to the high level of formal medical training and clinical expertise of the lay reporters, who were able to accurately diagnose and report neurological conditions without supervision.
Evidence: Eight male and female Ugandan lay mHealth reporters, age 20–30 years, were recruited and trained.

The training covered basic concepts of health and disease, the purpose and content of the program, clinical and social aspects of NS, use and maintenance of the equipment ... questionnaire presentation, data collection and submission

Physical examination of the sample of newly reported and existing NS cases confirmed a diagnosis of NS. On examination, children newly reported by mHealth workers to have NS were found to have longstanding disease.
mHealth reporters were pilot-tested and evaluated for satisfactory performance before they returned to their respective villages to commence weekly reporting.
230.Rationale: The reporters are lay persons with minimal training, not highly trained medical experts. They were trained to use the system and administer surveys, not to diagnose conditions. Diagnosis confirmation was done by medical professionals. The success of data collection is attributed to the system design, training, and support, not the reporters' clinical expertise.

231.Option: The high proportion of multibacillary (MB) leprosy cases (up to 74.3% in 2019) suggests that the current leprosy elimination strategies in Yunnan are failing to prevent the progression of early-stage disease to advanced forms.
Evidence: Among the new cases of leprosy detected, the percentage of multibacillary (MB) cases was relatively high in most of the years investigated, ranging from 59.0% in 2008 to 74.3% in 2019 (Table 1).
early detection increased from 53.9% in time period 1 to 60.3% in time period 2 (P<0.01).
231.Rationale: The passage reports a high proportion of MB cases but does not interpret this as a failure of strategies. In fact, early detection rates improved, indicating some success in identifying cases earlier. The high MB proportion could be due to factors such as detection bias or the natural history of the disease as overall incidence declines, not necessarily strategy failure.

231.Option: The observed reduction in the number of newly detected leprosy cases after 2004 can be explained by socioeconomic development, improved access to health services, an effective surveillance system, better travel opportunities, and improved education for the population.
Evidence: After many years of intensive effort, leprosy has been effectively controlled in China through the implementation of effective strategies and measures, including sustainable support and investment from the government, the establishment of an effective surveillance system, management of leprosy patients, and treatment of leprosy, first with dapsone (DDS) monotherapy and then, after 1990, with multidrug treatment (MDT).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.
231.Rationale: The passage attributes the reduction to specific public health interventions (MDT, special fund, surveillance) rather than broad socioeconomic factors. While improved access to health services is implied through investments in transport, 'better travel opportunities' and 'improved education for the population' are not mentioned as explanatory factors. The inference mixes supported and unsupported elements.

231.Option: The continued presence of leprosy in 17 highly endemic counties indicates that multidrug treatment (MDT) has been ineffective in preventing transmission in remote, mountainous regions of Yunnan.
Evidence: Despite the declaration of the elimination of leprosy at the national level in 1998 (<1/100,000), leprosy remains prevalent in some of the difficult-to-access mountain regions of Southwest China.
Currently, 17/117 (14.5%) of the counties remain leprosy-endemic regions.
Since 1990, MDT has been used to treat leprosy in every county in Yunnan.
With the introduction of MDT, the number of newly detected leprosy patients declined significantly.
231.Rationale: The persistence of leprosy in remote counties is noted, but the passage does not attribute this to ineffectiveness of MDT. Instead, it highlights geographical and access challenges. MDT is described as having contributed to significant declines overall, so its effectiveness is supported.

231.Option: The increase in early detection rates from 53.9% to 60.3% between time periods 1 and 2 suggests that contact examination and health education have been the primary drivers of the reduction in leprosy burden in Yunnan.
Evidence: early detection increased from 53.9% in time period 1 to 60.3% in time period 2 (P<0.01).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.
231.Rationale: While contact examination and health education are components of early detection strategies, the passage does not identify them as the primary drivers of the overall reduction in leprosy burden. The reduction is attributed more broadly to MDT and the special fund, which impact transmission and treatment.

231.Option: The substantial decline in leprosy case detection and the reduction in leprosy-endemic counties in Yunnan Province over the past 30 years can be attributed to the implementation of multidrug treatment (MDT), the establishment of a special fund for leprosy, and the development of a comprehensive surveillance system.
Evidence: leprosy has been effectively controlled in China through the implementation of effective strategies and measures, including sustainable support and investment from the government, the establishment of an effective surveillance system, management of leprosy patients, and treatment of leprosy, first with dapsone (DDS) monotherapy and then, after 1990, with multidrug treatment (MDT).
Since 1990, MDT has been used to treat leprosy in every county in Yunnan. In 2004, a special fund was established by the central government to combat leprosy in the region.
With the introduction of MDT, the number of newly detected leprosy patients declined significantly.

After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2.
231.Rationale: The passage directly links the implementation of MDT, the establishment of the special fund, and the existence of an effective surveillance system to the decline in leprosy cases and endemic counties. These factors are presented as key components of the control strategy that led to the observed reductions.

232.Option: The high proportion of rabies cases in children under 15 years of age suggests that the current surveillance system is more effective at detecting cases in younger populations.
Evidence: of the 14 cases of human rabies, 12 were children below 15 years of age
232.Rationale: The passage shows a high proportion of rabies cases in children, which is consistent with global epidemiology, but there is no evidence linking this to surveillance effectiveness. The investigation revealed cases missed by routine surveillance, and the proportion reflects risk factors like exposure, not detection bias.

232.Option: The absence of rabies cases in the routine surveillance system during the outbreak indicates that the surveillance system is functioning effectively by excluding false positives.
Evidence: Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
232.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that confirmed rabies cases were not captured by routine surveillance, which indicates under-reporting and surveillance failure, not effective exclusion of false positives. No evidence supports that the system is functioning effectively; rather, it highlights deficiencies.

232.Option: The poor quality of records at Prophylaxis Centers is the primary reason for the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
Evidence: Only 72 (8.7%) of animal bite victims had an animal bite investigation form filled out and the majority of these forms were incomplete.
Information on post-exposure vaccination was available in only 19.0% (156/819) of victims.
232.Rationale: While poor record-keeping is documented, the passage does not establish it as the primary reason for under-reporting. Other factors, such as clinical misdiagnosis, lack of health professional awareness, and failures in case notification, are also emphasized, and no causal primacy is assigned to record quality.

232.Option: The failure to diagnose rabies in clinical settings, combined with a lack of clinical awareness among health professionals, contributes to the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
Evidence: During the community investigation, an additional two cases admitted in August to a different hospital in Maputo were identified. Rabies was not suspected and for this reason, they were not initially recorded as rabies.
In Matola, one victim attended a health center and received only tetanus vaccine and the parents were informed that it was sufficient.
In Maputo City, of ten victims who attended health centers, nine were given only tetanus vaccine and parents were told that it was sufficient. They were not referred to the Prophylaxis Center.
232.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of rabies cases not being suspected in clinical settings, leading to missed diagnoses. Health professionals demonstrated lack of awareness by providing inadequate post-exposure prophylaxis and failing to refer bite victims. These factors contribute to under-reporting as cases are not recognized or reported to surveillance systems.

232.Option: None of the cases of human rabies that occurred during the outbreak were notified through the routine surveillance system, showing that rabies is seriously under-reported.
Evidence: Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
232.Rationale: This statement is directly supported by the passage. The fact that no confirmed cases were notified through routine surveillance is a clear epidemiological indicator of under-reporting, implying that the surveillance system is not capturing actual cases.

233.Option: The failure to sustain a Th17 response beyond 14 days in the murine model indicates that this subset is not essential for long-term fungal control, as its decline coincides with disease remission.
Evidence: The numbers of Th17 cells (CD4hi IL-17+) rose sharply during the early stages of the disease (until 14 days after infection) and fell gradually during later time points in both footpad (Fig 1D and S3B Fig) and dLN (Fig 1D and S4B Fig).

Taking into consideration that this is a self-healing animal model of CBM, these results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.
233.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that Th17 cells decline after 14 days and disease remission occurs, but it does not indicate that Th17 is not essential for long-term control. In fact, neutralization of IL-17A impaired early fungal elimination, indicating its crucial role in the immune response. The decline is part of a coordinated transition to Th1 dominance, not evidence of non-essentiality.

233.Option: The initial Th17 response in the footpad is primarily responsible for the recruitment of neutrophils, which are essential for the early elimination of fungal conidia and hyphae, while Th1 cells become critical later for clearing muriform cells.
233.Rationale: The Passage Body does not provide evidence linking Th17 response to neutrophil recruitment or specifying that Th1 cells clear muriform cells. While fungal morphotypes (conidia, hyphae, muriform cells) are mentioned, their clearance by specific T cell subsets is not discussed. The experimental findings focus on T cell populations and fungal burden without detailing effector mechanisms like neutrophil recruitment.

233.Option: The coordinated action of CD4+ T lymphocyte subpopulations, with initial Th17 polarization followed by Th1 dominance and Treg involvement, is essential for effective fungal clearance and disease remission in experimental chromoblastomycosis.
Evidence: Taking into consideration that this is a self-healing animal model of CBM, these results indicate that the increase in the Th17 population in the infection site during the first 14 days, followed by Th1 population increase, closely assisted by a growing Treg population until disease resolution, may represent the ideal balance of these populations when aiming at CBM remission.
Neutralization of IL-17A and IFN-γ impairs the elimination of fungal cells in the course of murine CBM.

Depletion of CD25 + cells reduced CFUs in the footpad at day 14 post-infection but delayed disease resolution, reflected by a reduction in fungal burden after 14 days of infection. Furthermore, fungal cells were not cleared in later stages of the disease, prolonging CBM clinical features in those animals.
233.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that the coordinated balance of Th17, Th1, and Treg populations is key to disease remission in the self-healing murine model. Experimental manipulations such as neutralization of IL-17A or IFN-γ and depletion of CD25+ cells impair fungal clearance and delay resolution, supporting the essential role of these CD4+ T cell subsets in effective immune response.

233.Option: The chronic inflammatory process observed in human CBM patients may be due to an unbalanced immune response that fails to transition from Th17 to Th1 dominance, resulting in persistent fungal burden and inadequate regulation by Treg cells.
Evidence: In situ studies on CBM patients, as well as in experimental models, show a correlation between Th2 response and disease severity, together with a high fungal load. The milder form of the disease is related to a Th1 profile, with high IFN-γ production, lower levels of IL-10, and lower fungal burden.
233.Rationale: While the Passage Body mentions that in human CBM, Th1 response is associated with mild disease and Th2 with severity, it does not provide direct evidence linking the Th17 to Th1 transition observed in the murine model to chronic inflammation in human patients. The experimental findings are specific to the murine model and do not address human chronicity in terms of Th17/Th1 balance; the inference requires extrapolation beyond the provided evidence.

234.Option: The identification of genotypes previously described in animals suggests potential zoonotic and anthroponotic transmission of Enterocytozoon bieneusi in Mozambique.
Evidence: Genotypes Type IV, S2, and Peru11 are 'previously reported in humans'.
Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the novel genotype HhMzEB1 clustered within Group 1.
Groups 1 and 2 comprise genotypes able to infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are, therefore, considered potentially zoonotic.
234.Rationale: While the novel genotype clusters in Group 1, which includes genotypes with zoonotic potential, the passage does not state that the specific genotypes identified (Type IV, S2, Peru11) have been previously described in animals. They are explicitly noted as previously reported in humans. Therefore, the claim that these genotypes are 'previously described in animals' is not supported by the passage, and thus the inference about zoonotic and anthroponotic transmission cannot be directly derived from the evidence provided.

234.Option: The detection of E. bieneusi genotypes in both rural and urban settings suggests that transmission is primarily driven by environmental contamination rather than animal contact.
Evidence: Three out of four children investigated lived in rural areas.
All these children lived in rural areas.
234.Rationale: The passage describes study populations predominantly from rural settings (Zambézia and Maputo provinces), with no explicit mention of urban settings. Therefore, the premise of detection in both rural and urban settings is not supported. Additionally, the inference that transmission is primarily driven by environmental contamination rather than animal contact is not derived from the evidence; the passage mentions multiple transmission routes (contaminated food/water, contact with feces or contaminated soils) but does not prioritize one over the other based on setting.

234.Option: The high genetic diversity of E. bieneusi in Mozambique, as evidenced by the identification of a novel genotype, suggests that the parasite is undergoing rapid evolution in this region.
Evidence: a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1)
Novel genotype HhMzEb1 differed from genotype D (AF101200) by one nucleotide.
234.Rationale: The identification of a novel genotype does not, by itself, demonstrate 'high genetic diversity' or 'rapid evolution.' The passage reports four genotypes from nine positive cases, but does not contextualize this as high diversity relative to other regions. No evidence is provided regarding evolutionary rates, such as comparative data over time, so the inference about rapid evolution is not derivable from the passage.

234.Option: The presence of multiple E. bieneusi genotypes in both symptomatic and asymptomatic children indicates that clinical symptoms are not reliably associated with specific genotypes.
Evidence: The prevalence for those without symptoms was 0.9% (8/916, 95% CI: 0.4–1.7) and for those with symptoms 0.3% (1/331, 95% CI: 0.04–2.1), and those with diarrhoea 0.4% (1/251, 95% CI: 0.06–2.8).
In the Zambézia province, E. bieneusi was found in 3 asymptomatic children.
In Maputo province, E. bieneusi was found in six male children, one presenting with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 5 asymptomatic.
Among the 4 boys infected with the novel genotype, HhMzEb1, one was HIV positive, presented with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea...
The S2 and Type IV genotypes were found in a single boy each...
Nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).
234.Rationale: The evidence shows that E. bieneusi infections were found in both symptomatic and asymptomatic children, with multiple genotypes (Type IV, S2, Peru11, HhMzEb1) detected. Importantly, the novel genotype HhMzEb1 was found in both a symptomatic child and asymptomatic children, while other genotypes were found only in asymptomatic children. This distribution indicates that the presence or absence of clinical symptoms is not consistently linked to specific genotypes, supporting the inference that symptoms are not reliably associated with particular genotypes.

234.Option: The fact that all identified genotypes belong to Group 1 implies that E. bieneusi in Mozambique is primarily transmitted through direct human-to-human contact.
Evidence: Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the novel genotype HhMzEB1 clustered within Group 1.
Groups 1 and 2 comprise genotypes able to infect a broad range of mammalian species including humans and are, therefore, considered potentially zoonotic.
234.Rationale: The passage only explicitly states that the novel genotype HhMzEB1 belongs to Group 1; it does not assign the other genotypes (Type IV, S2, Peru11) to any phylogenetic group. Therefore, the claim that all identified genotypes belong to Group 1 is not supported. Moreover, Group 1 genotypes are described as potentially zoonotic, implying transmission between animals and humans, not exclusively human-to-human transmission. Thus, the inference is not derivable from the evidence.

235.Option: The successful completion of five rounds of MDA in Sri Lanka, combined with high MDA compliance rates, ensures that transmission assessment surveys are sufficient to confirm the interruption of lymphatic filariasis transmission.
Evidence: While TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs
community CFA rates were <2% in 17 of 19 PHIs, but upper confidence limits for CFA were >2% in 5 of 19 PHIs
235.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TAS cannot guarantee transmission interruption, and results from comprehensive surveillance show persistent filarial DNA in mosquitoes and antigenemia in some PHI areas, despite high MDA compliance and completion of five rounds. This contradicts the sufficiency of TAS for confirming transmission interruption.

235.Option: The high rates of antifilarial antibodies in primary school children across most PHIs suggest that transmission assessment surveys are highly sensitive for detecting ongoing filariasis transmission in endemic areas.
Evidence: Blood samples were tested for filarial antigenemia by card test
CFA rates were very low in children tested in school surveys, and this was consistent with TAS results.
Antibody rates exceeded the target rate of 2% in 10 of 19 PHIs
235.Rationale: TAS is based on detecting circulating filarial antigenemia (CFA), not antibodies. High antibody rates in school children indicate past exposure or immune response, but do not directly correlate with current transmission detection by TAS. TAS results showed low CFA rates, and the passage does not link antibody rates to TAS sensitivity for ongoing transmission.

235.Option: The fact that none of the children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia indicates that transmission assessment surveys are reliable indicators of the absence of active filarial transmission.
Evidence: none of the 16 children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs
While TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs)
235.Rationale: The absence of microfilaremia in CFA-positive children does not confirm the absence of active transmission, as molecular xenodiagnosis detected filarial DNA in mosquitoes in several PHIs, indicating potential ongoing transmission. The passage also states that TAS cannot guarantee transmission interruption, undermining its reliability as a sole indicator.

235.Option: The detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes in some PHIs suggests that transmission assessment surveys are overly conservative and should be abandoned in favor of molecular xenodiagnosis for all post-MDA evaluations.
Evidence: While TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted
The present study provided an opportunity to gain further experience with these parameters in the post-MDA setting.
The second aim was to assess the relative value of different methods for detecting low level persistence of filariasis after MDA.
235.Rationale: The passage acknowledges TAS utility for MDA decisions and does not suggest abandoning TAS. Instead, it emphasizes complementary surveillance with methods like molecular xenodiagnosis to detect low-level persistence, indicating that multiple approaches are needed, not a replacement of TAS.

235.Option: While transmission assessment surveys may be useful for decisions regarding stopping mass drug administration, they are not sufficient to show that lymphatic filariasis transmission has been interrupted, as evidenced by the detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes and persistent antigenemia in adults in areas that passed TAS.
Evidence: While TAS results may be useful for deciding whether MDA can be stopped, TAS cannot guarantee that LF transmission has been interrupted in evaluation units (EUs), which are typically districts with populations that may exceed 1 million.
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs
community CFA rates were <2% in 17 of 19 PHIs, but upper confidence limits for CFA were >2% in 5 of 19 PHIs
All EUs “passed” TAS including the coastal Galle District EU, where high rates for filariasis markers were noted in two PHI study areas.
235.Rationale: The passage directly states that TAS is not sufficient to guarantee transmission interruption. Comprehensive surveillance in PHI areas revealed persistent filarial DNA in mosquitoes and antigenemia in adults, even in EUs that passed TAS, demonstrating that TAS alone cannot confirm elimination and that additional methods are needed to assess low-level persistence.

236.Option: The integration of mHealth tools with community health volunteers enables the collection of high-quality photographic data for therapeutic response assessment, which is as accurate as in-person evaluation and supports the scalability of digital health interventions in rural settings.
Evidence: This evaluation strategy has been found to be sensitive and specific, relative to in-person assessment [12].
The app allowed CHV to monitor the clinical characteristics and evolution of the lesions, and to take and transmit photographs.
236.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that photographic evaluation is sensitive and specific compared to in-person assessment, and that the app enables CHVs to collect photographic data. However, the passage does not explicitly describe the data as 'high-quality,' and while it mentions that mHealth tools offer opportunities to overcome barriers in this specific setting, it does not provide evidence or reasoning to support the scalability of digital health interventions in other rural settings. Scalability is not addressed or derived from the passage body.

236.Option: The use of a mobile health app by community health volunteers significantly improved the proportion of patients monitored at target study week 26 compared to standard institution-based follow-up, despite challenges related to connectivity and security.
Evidence: Among these, 26/49 (53.1%) were evaluated by active follow-up at this time in the intervention arm, and none (0/25, 0%) of those in the randomized control arm who, according to the standard-of-care were instructed to return for evaluation at week 13 and 26 (difference = 53.1%, 95% confidence interval 39.1–67.0%, p<0.001).
Travel to the town of Tumaco, where the health centers are located, may take up to 8 hours, which restricts access to health services.
Armed conflict has contributed to internal population displacement.
Inclusion criteria: ... b) being safe for work to be conducted
236.Rationale: The passage directly reports a statistically significant improvement in follow-up at week 26 with the mHealth app compared to standard care, as evidenced by the p-value and confidence interval. The setting describes significant challenges such as long travel times and security concerns due to armed conflict, which contextualize the remote rural setting with limited infrastructure.
